intro: February 2008. You run Yahoo, still one of the most-visited destinations on the internet. Microsoft just put $44.6 billion on the table to buy you — a huge premium over your sagging stock. Your co-founder thinks you're worth far more.

realOutcome.valuation: core business sold to Verizon for $4.8B in 2016
realOutcome.summary: Yahoo turned down a $44.6B offer and later sold its core for about a tenth of that. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: A tiny, fast-growing social network called Facebook is willing to sell for around $1 billion. Its 22-year-old founder is on the fence, and your instinct is that the price is too high for a college website.

choices.text: Lowball to ~$850M — it's just a college site.
choices.reveal: Yahoo agreed to ~$1B, then tried to cut the price to $850M after a bad earnings quarter. Zuckerberg walked. Facebook is now worth over a thousand times that.

choices.text: Pay the full $1B and close it cleanly.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You own the social graph before it explodes. Yahoo becomes the home of the social web instead of a fading portal.

choices.text: Offer $2B to make the deal impossible to refuse.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You overpay by the standards of 2006 and look reckless — for about a year, until it becomes the best acquisition in tech history.

decisions.scenario: Microsoft's formal offer: $44.6 billion, roughly $31 a share, a giant premium. It's the deal of a lifetime or a humiliating surrender, depending on who you ask.

choices.text: Reject it. Yahoo is worth far more than $31.
choices.reveal: Yahoo rejected Microsoft's $44.6B offer as too low. The stock never came close to $31 again, and the company was eventually sold for a fraction of it.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You hand shareholders a massive premium at the exact top. In hindsight it was the single best price Yahoo would ever be offered.

choices.text: Negotiate to about $35 a share and close.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Microsoft reportedly would have gone higher. You squeeze out even more and still get out at the peak. Everyone wins but the Bing team.

decisions.scenario: Having spurned Microsoft, you need a growth story. Google offers to power your search ads — an easy revenue boost that leans on your biggest competitor.

choices.text: Sign the search-ad partnership with Google.
choices.reveal: Yahoo struck a search deal with Google to prove it didn't need Microsoft. Regulators threatened an antitrust suit and the deal collapsed, leaving Yahoo with nothing and no Microsoft offer to return to.

choices.text: Go back and close the Microsoft deal after all.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You swallow your pride while the offer is still warm. Shareholders get their premium and Yahoo's decline never happens.

choices.text: Double down and reinvest in your own search.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Hard and expensive, but you keep the one asset — search data — that actually compounds. Yahoo stays a real technology company.

decisions.scenario: Your new CEO wants to stop spending on search entirely. Microsoft offers a 10-year deal to run Yahoo's search behind the scenes with Bing.

choices.text: Hand search to Microsoft's Bing for a decade.
choices.reveal: Yahoo outsourced its search to Bing in 2009, giving up its own search technology and data for short-term savings. It surrendered the exact business that made Google a giant.

choices.text: Keep search in-house and reinvest in it.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Owning your search data keeps you in the only game that matters online. You stay an internet infrastructure company, not a content site renting its brain.

choices.text: Spin search into a joint venture you control.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You get help with the costs while keeping the strategic asset and the upside. A middle path that doesn't amputate your future.

decisions.scenario: A new CEO arrives promising a turnaround. The realistic options: keep buying flashy startups, or admit that your stake in Alibaba is now worth more than all of Yahoo itself.

choices.text: Buy Tumblr for $1.1B and coast on Alibaba.
choices.reveal: Yahoo bought Tumblr for $1.1B in 2013 and later wrote nearly all of it off. The company became a holding vehicle for its Alibaba stake with a struggling website attached.

choices.text: Spin off the Alibaba stake and return the cash.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You stop pretending the core business is the value and hand shareholders the Alibaba windfall cleanly. It's the honest, higher-value move.

choices.text: Refocus everything on one product you can win.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Instead of buying attention, you rebuild one franchise — mail, or finance, or sports — into something people choose. Focus beats acquisition sprees.

bands.line: You sold high or bought the future. Villain episode: WON.
Diminished but standing — a real company, not a holding vehicle.
Turned down $44.6B, sold for $4.8B. Faithful to history. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Microsoft's spurned bid looks like a gift now. Today's agents flagged a company turning down its best exit →
